substrate: complex Intent field, focusing-saturating on-site nonlinearity (Phase-2)
absolute_time_note: varying quantity is probe phase velocity (cells/absolute tick); the tick is universal, no clock perturbed
verdict: A localized high-Intent core (the 'mass') DOES create an index well: n-1 peaks at 0.7275, so a passing probe wave slows and would refract TOWARD the core — Q2's mechanism (gravity bends light) emerges from the substrate, faithful to absolute time (it is the probe's PHASE VELOCITY, cells-per-absolute-tick, that drops; no clock is touched). BUT THE RANGE: the well is best fit by sech2 (short-range) (sech^2 residual 1.91e-01 vs 1/r residual 7.01e+00); tail falls 2011493340.64x over a 4x distance span vs the 4.0x a 1/r field would give -> short-range (steeper than 1/r — tracks the core). So the emergent field is SHORT-RANGE (it tracks the core profile), NOT the long-range 1/r that Newtonian gravity requires. PRODUCTIVE NEGATIVE (pre-registered prediction confirmed): the substrate's own nonlinearity refracts light at a mass, but as a contact-range index bump, not a 1/r potential. Recovering 1/r — and only then asking the factor-of-2 question — is UNSOLVED: it needs a long-range mediator (a massless mode / the field's far tail / a collective effect), not a bare soliton core. This is the real gap between 'refracts light' and 'is gravity'. FDTD cross-check: measured extra phase / analytic = None (should be ~1 if the linearized index is honest). Bucket 0 unchanged.
